content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Whenever someone mentioned Redis, I immediately thought of caching, session storage, or rate limiting. That’s probably how most of us learned Redis.
But while exploring AI infrastructure recently, I came across Redis Iris, and it made me realize that Redis is trying to solve a completely different problem now.
The biggest challenge with AI agents isn’t always the LLM. It’s context.
An agent needs to remember previous conversations, understand user preferences, access live business data, and avoid asking the same question repeatedly. Without context, even the smartest model starts every interaction from scratch.
That’s exactly where Redis Iris fits in.
Instead of being “just a cache,” Redis Iris is designed as a Context Engine for AI agents. Its goal is to give agents fast, fresh, and relevant context so they can make better decisions.
The features that stood out to me
🧠 Agent Memory
This gives AI agents both short-term and long-term memory. Instead of forgetting everything after one conversation, agents can remember previous interactions, user preferences, and important decisions across sessions.
⚡ LangCache
One feature I found particularly interesting is semantic caching. Instead of sending nearly identical prompts to an LLM every time, LangCache detects similar requests and returns a cached response. That means lower API costs and much faster responses.
🔍 Context Retriever
Rather than letting an agent query databases directly, Redis generates structured tools that agents can safely use to retrieve business data. It provides controlled, schema-aware access instead of relying on custom integrations for every application.
🔄 Data Integration
Business data changes constantly. Redis Data Integration keeps Redis synchronized with databases like PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, and SQL Server, so AI agents always work with fresh information instead of stale snapshots.
Where would I use it?
If I were building a customer support agent, I wouldn’t want it to forget users after every conversation. I’d use Agent Memory.
If thousands of users ask the same questions every day, LangCache could reduce LLM calls and save a significant amount on API costs.
If the agent needs customer orders or ticket information, I’d use Context Retriever instead of giving it direct database access.
And if that customer data changes frequently, Data Integration would ensure the agent always sees the latest information.
The more I learn about AI systems, the more I realize that the challenge isn’t building a smarter model it’s building better infrastructure around it.
Redis was once something I associated only with caching.
Now, I see it evolving into a platform that provides memory, context, and real-time data for AI agents.
If you’re building production AI applications, Redis Iris is definitely worth exploring.
